title: India’s Unconvincing Economic Facade
gist: Derek Scissors argues India's claim to be the world's fastest-growing major economy rests on unreliable statistics, with weak trade competitiveness, foreign-investor flight, hidden mass unemployment, and self-defeating land and labor policies that squander a one-time demographic window.

insights.claim: India's official FY2026 real GDP growth of 7.7 percent is undermined by nominal GDP growing only 8.9 percent (slowest since the COVID contraction) and a GDP deflator smaller than the deflators of GDP's major components, suggesting real growth is exaggerated.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: If headline real growth is inflated by a manipulated deflator, India equity and macro theses built on the growth-leader narrative are mispriced.
insights.quote: the GDP deflator was somehow smaller than the implicit deflators for the major components of GDP

insights.claim: Physical and financial indicators contradict rapid growth: state-provided power supply rose only 1 percent in FY2026, and 'discrepancies' have at times contributed unreasonably large shares to reported GDP growth.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Low power-demand growth is a hard-to-fake real-activity signal that flatly contradicts the official growth story.
insights.quote: Power supplied by state providers, which are dominant in the industry, rose only 1 percent in fiscal year 2026.

insights.claim: Domestic capital is voting no confidence: over 800 million people still received free food in 2025, gold imports jumped 24 percent to $72 billion, and the policy rate has declined sizably over the decade with low rates promised, patterns typical of slow-growing aging economies.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Surging gold demand and a multi-year rate-cutting path signal weak domestic confidence inconsistent with a booming economy.
insights.quote: gold imports jumping 24 percent to $72 billion

insights.claim: The rupee has hit annual lows against the dollar for eight straight years, so India's dollar GDP barely more than doubled from FY2012-FY2024 versus a comparable ~600 percent increase for China at an equivalent development stage.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the 'India is the new China' thesis on a dollar basis, relevant to allocators rotating EM exposure into India.
insights.quote: India is beaten soundly on a local currency basis and far worse in dollars, due to the rupee's decline.

insights.claim: India's reported 3.1 percent unemployment relies on a definition counting anyone who worked even one hour in a reference week as employed, with 30 such days qualifying a person as employed for the year.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Exposes the headline jobless rate as nearly meaningless, undercutting the demographic-dividend narrative.
insights.quote: a person was considered as unemployed in a week if he/she did not work even for 1 hour on any day during the reference week

insights.claim: Employment quality is poor: in 2025 self-employment exceeded 62 percent, casual labor was another 23 percent, standard wage-salary work only 14 percent of the core labor force, and unincorporated firms averaged about 1.6 workers.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Reveals that counted 'jobs' are largely subsistence work unable to support a decent life, a structural drag on consumption.
insights.quote: Standard wage-salary employment is only 14 percent of the core labor force.

insights.claim: The government says India needs nearly eight million new nonfarm jobs annually, yet agriculture's labor share held at 42 percent from 2017 to 2025 and over five million manufacturing jobs were lost at unincorporated firms.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Manufacturing job losses and stalled structural shift signal the 'India as factory of the world' thesis is not materializing.
insights.quote: official data showed over five million manufacturing jobs lost at unincorporated firms

insights.claim: India has only one generation of growth-friendly demographics (median age rising from 21.2 in 2000 toward a projected 38.3 by 2050) and fertility has already fallen below replacement, yet it is failing to capitalize during this window.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Frames the present as a closing, non-repeatable opportunity, raising the stakes of policy failure for long-horizon investors.
insights.quote: India has one and only one generation of demographic conditions conducive to rapid growth

insights.claim: India's non-oil goods trade deficit set successive records in FY2023, FY2025 and FY2026, and its share of global manufacturing exports was just 1.8 percent in 2024 despite holding nearly 10 times that share of world population.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: The world's largest labor force is not translating into export competitiveness, contradicting the rising-competitiveness thesis.
insights.quote: India had a 1.8 percent share of global manufacturing exports in 2024

insights.claim: Textiles, a supposed Indian comparative advantage, grew exports only 2 percent in FY2026 and remain below FY2022 in dollar terms, even after the rupee's real effective exchange rate fell to spring-2014 levels.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Failure to gain share even with a cheap currency signals deep structural, not cyclical, competitiveness problems.
insights.quote: exports inched only 2 percent higher in fiscal 2026. In dollar terms, they're lower than in fiscal 2022

insights.claim: Net inward FDI plunged to almost nothing since 2020 and net of portfolio flows capital left India in FY2026, with fewer than 150 new foreign company registrations in 2024 and 2025 combined and net portfolio investment hitting a decade low in early FY2027.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Concrete capital-flow evidence that global investors are net exiting India, a direct contrarian signal against bullish positioning.
insights.quote: On a net basis including portfolio flows, capital left India in fiscal 2026.

insights.claim: Singapore leads as both the largest source of inward and recipient of outward direct investment, raising the question of how much inbound FDI is merely recycled capital bringing no innovation, while less than a quarter of gross FDI is in manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Suggests headline gross FDI overstates genuine productive foreign investment in India.
insights.quote: Singapore is a global center for capital cycling, raising the question of how much of the money entering India is just returning capital

insights.claim: India's late-2025 reform raising the firing-permission threshold from 100 to 300 employees will merely shift bunching to firms of 299 workers, perpetuating a powerful disincentive against building the truly large firms needed for export-scale manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural cap on firm scale that will keep India from competing as a manufacturing exporter.
insights.quote: The result will be many firms with 299 employees rather than 99.

insights.claim: Poor labor absorption paralyzes land reform because firms hire less than ideal to keep payroll flexibility, leaving surplus rural laborers stuck on tiny holdings averaging barely over one hectare with 800 million people still on free food.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Links labor rigidity to chronic rural poverty, explaining why productivity-raising land consolidation stays blocked.
insights.quote: Poor labor absorption has crushing implications.

insights.claim: India runs deficits exceeding 4 percent of GDP with central government liabilities more than tripling from FY2016 to FY2026, nearly a quarter of FY2026 spending funded by borrowing and a fifth going to interest, with credit growth near 16 percent outpacing nominal GDP.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: Fiscal trajectory of a slow-growth rich country, a red flag for sovereign risk and the durability of the growth claim.
insights.quote: Nearly one-fourth of spending in fiscal 2026 was funded by borrowing, and one-fifth went to interest payments.

insights.claim: Scissors concludes India claims growth leadership and strong fundamentals but the data show manipulated employment statistics, weak trade competitiveness and foreign-investor distrust, with policy discouraging hiring at scale while spending like a slow-growth rich country.
insights.speaker: Derek Scissors
insights.so_what: The overarching short/skeptic thesis: treat India's reported data with low reliability when sizing exposure.
insights.quote: The policy set also fits an India pretending to be more successful than it actually is
